quoted_tweet.text:
I often say that price says everything, but there are sometimes anomalies in small caps. Check the case study of $URGN, which we recommended buying at $10 after it passed a Phase 3 trial. 

By the way, our base-case scenario now, after discussions with management, is that it gets bought out between $40–$50 after it can show a few quarters of growth.

kept.text:
$URGN 
Strong Q1 results!
➢ Revenue: $50.96m, +152% YoY (vs. $44.82m est.) ✅
➢ EPS: -$0.47 (vs. -$0.48 est.) ✅
➢ Zusduri revenue: $29.2m, +109% QoQ ✅

Zusduri KPIs (QoQ comparison)
➢ Activated sites of care: 838 ➔ 972 (+134 / +16%) 
➢ Unique prescribers: 102 ➔ 256 (+154 / +151%) 
➢ Repeat prescribers: 32 ➔ 103 (+71 / +221%)

With $100 m in Jelmyto sales for 2026 according to guidance, the current Zusduri run-rate of ~$30M per quarter already puts us at $220m in revenue for 2026. 

However, since Zusduri will continue to scale in the coming quarters, I think $290m – $300m in total 2026 revenue is possible. For comparison: the current analyst consensus sits at $245m for 2026. 

Meaning: Analysts should be significantly raising their estimates following this report. 
Let's see how the stock reacts later. I will share my main takeaways from the earnings call later this evening.

quoted_tweet.text:
I'm not exactly sure what’s driving the chart momentum behind $URGN right now, but I’m certainly not complaining about it 😅

​Stock is already up more than 20% since that weird earnings sell-off in early March.

​Btw: The urologist survey highlighted in this post (https://t.co/sgS1kjcyqG) is a great read. Respondents believe Zusduri, Urogen’s flagship drug, could reach a 35% peak market share (vs. 20% company guidance), which would increase the peak sales potential with the current drug pipeline to nearly $2b ($5b x 0.35 + $0.15b Jelmyto sales).

kept.text:
$URGN 
My thoughts on the Q1/26 numbers and summary of the earnings call 🧵

➢ At first, I was extremely frustrated with the market reaction (from +15% pre-market to 0%). Meanwhile, the market seems to have come to its senses, with the stock currently up +10%. Given the magnitude of the revenue beat and the resulting implications for FY 2026 revenue (in my view, analysts' revenue estimates should be raised by 15-20%), the share price gain IMO should have been even higher.

➢ In my view, the Zusduri thesis has been significantly de-risked today. Much was already clear beforehand: 
▪️ UroGen has strong insurance coverage, 
▪️ already has established business relationships with most urologists and clinics treating LG-IR-NMIBC, 
▪️ has strong clinical data, even looking >1 year post-treatment, 
▪️ has a first-mover advantage of at least several years over competitor products still in the approval process.

➢ What was not clear, however: How fast the adoption rate would ramp up after receiving the J-Code in January. It was questionable: 
▪️ Are specialist practices and clinics willing to deviate from the standard of care, TURBT (surgical removal of tumor tissue from the bladder), and try a new form of treatment with Zusduri? 
▪️ Are small specialist practices, in particular, really willing to take on the bureaucracy that a buy-and-bill drug (i.e., paying upfront, subsequent billing effort with insurance companies, etc.) entails?

➢ After today's massive jump in revenue compared to Q4/25, where the J-Code was not yet active, both of these questions must, in my view, be answered with yes. Therefore, the thesis has been significantly de-risked for me personally today.

Now for the earnings call summary:
➢ As mentioned in the previous post, Zusduri grew by 109% QoQ. The observed growth trends "...were consistent throughout the quarter and not limited to the immediate period following the implementation of the J-Code." According to CEO Liz Barrett, this reinforces the expectation of continuous growth in Q2 and beyond.

➢ The timeframe from PEF (Patient Enrollment Form) to the start of treatment is continuously improving from the initial 45-60 days in Q4/25. Goal: 14-21 days, like with Jelmyto. This accelerates the revenue conversion.

➢ Insurance coverage already exists for 95% of covered lives, so there should be no obstacle to future growth here.

➢ Zusduri growth trends are in line with internal expectations. Target of >$1B peak sales for Zusduri was reaffirmed.

➢ About 70% of the relevant market for Zusduri is located in community practices. In Q4/25, the hospital/community revenue mix was 60/40; at the end of Q1, it was 50/50, and in the long term, it is expected to approach 30/70.

➢ Advantages of Zusduri over competing products: 
▪️ Primary chemoablation (tumor resolution) instead of supplemental treatment after surgery, 
▪️ short six-week therapy duration instead of treatment cycles lasting up to a year, 
▪️ uncomplicated outpatient application directly in the practice without an operating room, 
▪️ massive relief for patients by avoiding surgery and permanent follow-up care.

➢ An improved Zusduri formulation called UGN-103 is intended to be used for high-grade NMIBC and adjuvant therapies after surgery, in addition to the current LG-IR-NMIBC indication. Concepts for corresponding studies are being developed. Approval is planned for 2027, with the J-Code expected in early 2028.

➢ Q1/26 represents the high point for SG&A expenses at $51.5M due to one-time costs for debt refinancing and the build-up of marketing activities. The OPEX guide of $240m - $250m was confirmed.

➢ And now, in my view, one of the most important points:
 UroGen has compared Zusduri to Anktiva, a drug for high-grade NMIBC, in the past. When asked about this by an analyst, the CFO said that Anktiva saw a 220% jump in revenue 6 months after receiving its J-Code. Quote from the CFO: "...we're tracking ahead of that analog."

➢ Meaning: Zusduri generated $14m in revenue in Q4/25. A 220% increase would mean $44.8M in revenue for Zusduri for Q2/26 (after $29.2M in Q1/26). Since they are seeing faster growth than 220%, I would derive a revenue of $45m - $50m for Zusduri for Q2/26 from this.

➢ As I mentioned earlier, the $245m revenue estimates for FY 2026 are very likely significantly too low. Assuming $100m for Jelmyto, I calculate roughly $45m in Q2, $55m in Q3, and $65m in Q4 for Zusduri. This would result in $294m in revenue for FY 2026.

➢ Nice side effect of this revenue outlook: With a 92% gross margin, this would result in $270m in gross profit. Deducting $250M in CAPEX, UroGen would likely already be profitable by the end of the year.

➢ A side note: 5 out of 6 analysts on the call congratulated management on a great quarter. Based on my experience, I interpret this to mean that more than one estimate was beaten and that several estimate hikes should follow in the coming days.

My conclusion: 
Great numbers, currently my second-largest position (10% weighting). I am definitely staying invested. I wouldn't be surprised if there was a buyout by the end of the year. I am hoping for a price around $70 per share in case that happens.
